translations.translation:
Xiahou Dun (Yuanrang)
夏侯惇 (元讓)
Lived ADca.160–220
aSanguozhiBiographyRecords of the Three Kingdoms [三國志]
Scroll 9 (Wei 9)
byZL181
onDecember 5th, 2022

Xiahou Dun, styledYuanrang, was fromQiao,Pei commandery, and was a descendant ofXiahou Ying.
When he was fourteen, he studied under a teacher. There was someone who insulted the teacher, and[Xiahou] Dunkilled them. Thus, he was renowned for his fierce spirit.
When theGrand Progenitorfirst rose up,[Xiahou] Dunaccompanied him as a lieutenant general, following him in expeditions. When theGrand Progenitorwas actingGeneral who Exerts Might, he appointed[Xiahou] DunasMajorand had him garrison atBaima. He was later promoted to beColonel who Breaks and ChargesandGrand AdministratorofDong commandery.
When theGrand Progenitorlaunched an expedition againstTao Qian, he had[Xiahou] Dunremain behind to guardPuyang. WhenZhang Miaorebelled and welcomedLü Bu, the family of theGrand Progenitorwas inJuancheng.[Xiahou] Dunwent there with a light army and encountered[Lü] Bu. They fought each other.[Lü] Buwithdrew and enteredPuyang, seizing the army supplies of[Xiahou] Dun. They sent generals pretending to surrender, who together seized[Xiahou] Dun. They then demanded treasures and wealth. The army of[Xiahou] Dunwas in shock and terror, butHan Hao, a general under[Xiahou] Dun, led troops to guard the gates to the camp of[Xiahou] Dun. He summoned the army officers and various generals, and had all of them have their army units loosen their armor,1and to not act. The camp was then stabilized. He then went to where[Xiahou] Dunwas and scolded the hostage takers, saying, “You are all rebellious traitors, yet you dared to take a grand general hostage while also expecting to live! I have received orders to destroy traitors. How could I leave you be because of one general?” He then shed tears and told[Xiahou] Dun, “What else could I do under the national law?” He quickly summoned his soldiers to attack the hostage takers. The hostage takers were terrified and kowtowed, saying, “We just wanted to ask for money then leave, nothing more!”[Han] Haorebuked them several times and beheaded them all. Thus was[Xiahou] Dunreleased. When theGrand Progenitorheard of this, he told[Han] Hao, “Sir, what you did should be a law for tens of thousands of generations.” He thus ordered that from thereon, any hostage takers would all be attacked without consideration to the hostage. Thus, the taking of hostagesended.A•••
“Loosen their armor” [案甲] simply means to cease fighting in this case.↩
Sun Shengstates: According to theAnnals of theGlorious and Martial[Emperor], in the ninth year of theera [33], bandits kidnapped the full younger brother ofHonored LadyYin. Because the officials were not able to detain to compel the bandits, the bandits killed him. Afterwards, they would be attacked together, which would be a system of the past. However, during the [reigns of]Secure[Emperor]and theDeferential[Emperor], and after them, governance and moral influence declined. Hostage takers would not eschew princes and dukes, and none of thedirecting officialscould uphold the national rules, so when[Han] Haofirst beheaded them, he was accordingly praised by theMartial[Emperor] ofWei.
When theGrand Progenitorreturned fromXu province,[Xiahou] Dunfollowed on an expedition againstLü Bu. He was hit by a flying arrow and injured in his left eye.BHe served asGrand AdministratorofChenliuandGrand AdministratorofJiyin, and he also served asGeneral who Establishes Mightand enfeoffed asDistrict Marquisof Gao’an.
TheSummary of Weistates: At the time, whenXiahou Yuanand[Xiahou] Dunboth served as Generals, the army nicknamed[Xiahou] Dunthe “Blind Xiahou”.[Xiahou] Dunhated that, and whenever he saw a mirror, he would be furious and smash the mirror into the ground.
At the time, there was a severe drought and an outbreak of locusts.[Xiahou] Dunsevered the Taishou river to form a reservoir, personally carrying soil, and he ordered his officers to encourage the cultivation of rice. The common people would rely on such to benefit. He was transferred to beIntendantofHenan.
When theGrand Progenitorpacified theHebei Region, he [Xiahou Dun] served as the rear defense of the grand army. WhenYefell, he was madeGeneral Who Calms the Wavesand remained asIntendant, and he was permitted to handle matters as he deemed appropriate without being restricted by regulations.
In the twelfth year of theera [207], in consideration of all the achievements[Xiahou] Dunrendered, his fief was increased by one thousand and eight hundred households, joining with the previous for a total of two thousand and five hundred households
In the twenty-first year [216], he followed in the expedition againstSun Quan, and upon the return,[Xiahou] Dunwas instructed to supervise twenty-six armies, remaining inJuchao. He was gifted music dancers and renowned singers. The order stated:
“Wei Jiang had the achievement of being in harmony with the barbarians, and if he received music instruments of metal and stone for that,2is it not all the more for you,General?”•••
Wei Jiang [魏絳] was a minister of the State of Jin. He strived hard to promote harmony between the state and different ethnicities (“barbarians”), which resulted in peace and prosperity. He was rewarded with half the musicians and musical instruments, including metal bells and stone chimes, that were sent by the State of Zheng to the State of Jin.↩
In the twenty-fourth year [219], when the army of theGrand Progenitorwas atMobei,[Xiahou] Dunwas summoned, and he would often be in the same vehicle [as theGrand Progenitor]. He was treated with privilege and great favor, being able to leave or enter the bedroom [of theGrand Progenitor]. No other generals could compare to such regard. He was madeGeneral of the FrontCand supervised the armies to return toShouchun, then garrisoned atShaoling.
TheBook of Weistates: At the time, many generals received ranks and titles under theWei, with only[Xiahou] Dunhaving rank under theHan. He thus memorialized and explained that it was unbefitting for him to have the courtesy of not being regarded as a subject.3TheGrand Progenitorsaid, “I have heard that the best way to regard subjects is as teachers, and that the next best way to regard subjects is as friends; moreover, a subject is a noble and virtuous person. Trivial and insignificant is theWei, and to have you as its subject would be enough to degrade you, sir.”[Xiahou] Dunpersisted in his requests, and thus was madeGeneral of the Front.•••
It is not clear which courtXiahou Dunmemorialized to, but he likely petitioned to theWeicourt because he was not a subject to it, but a subject of theHancourt.Cao Caowas the King ofWei, but he andXiahou Dunwould have been equal to each other as they were both subjects of theHan.↩
After theCivil Emperorbecame King, he appointed[Xiahou] DunasGrand General, who died after several months.
***
Although[Xiahou] Dunwas in the military, he personally received teachers to receive learning. By nature, he was incorrupt and frugal. When he had surplus wealth, he would always donate them, and when he did not have enough, he would receive from the government. He would not cultivate his properties andbusiness.4•••
Xiahou Dunavoided enriching himself despite his status.↩
***
His posthumous title was theLoyalMarquis, and his son,[Xiahou] Chong, succeeded him. TheEmperor, in remembering the achievements of[Xiahou] Dun, and intended to make all his sons and grandsons marquises. He divided one thousand households from the fief[Xiahou] Dunand conferred it to seven sons and two grandsons to be enfeoff them all asMarquises within the Passes. The younger brother of[Xiahou] Dun,[Xiahou] Lian, and his son,[Xiahou] Mao, received their own fiefs asRanged Marquisesbeforehand. Previously, theGrand Progenitorhad hisdaughtermarry[Xiahou] Mao; she was thePrincessofQinghe.[Xiahou] Maowould becomePalace Attendant,Secretariat Minister,General Who Maintains the West, andGeneral who Guards the East, withacting tally.DWhen[Xiahou] Chongdied, his son,[Xiahou] Yi, succeeded him. When[Xiahou] Yidied, his son,[Xiahou] Shao, succeededhim.E
TheSummary of Weistates:[Xiahou] Mao, styledZilin, was the middle son of[Xiahou] Dun. When theCivil Emperorwas young, he and[Xiahou] Maowere close, so when he assumed the throne, he made himGeneral Who Maintains the West,bearing tally, and had him succeedXiahou Yuanin the command ofGuanzhong Basin.[Xiahou] Mao, by nature, had no military or strategic ability, but he was good at the family business. In the second year of theera [228], theBrightEmperorwent on a western expedition. There were people who had accusations against[Xiahou] Mao, who was then recalled to serve asSecretariat Minister. When[Xiahou] Maowas in the west, he collected many female performers and concubines, and hence, thePrincessand[Xiahou] Maowere not friendly with each other. Later, his younger brothers did not respect the rites and rules, so[Xiahou] Maooften reprimanded them harshly. His younger brothers feared punishment, so they all slandered and defamed[Xiahou] Mao, then told thePrincessto petition such. An edict arrested[Xiahou] Mao. TheEmperorwanted to kill him, but he asked theCommandant of the Chang River Encampments,Duan MoofJingzhao.[Duan] Mobelieved, “This is certainly due to thePrincessofQingheand[Xiahou] Maobeing unfriendly with each other, so she slandered and defamed him, hoping that you will not investigate the truth. Moreover, theGeneral Who Calms the Wavesjoined thedepartedEmperorin the achievement of stabilizing the realm. You should reconsider three times over.” TheEmperorwas enlightened, and he said, “I also thought so.” He thus released an edict, investigating who thePrincesswrote the petition for, and it turned out that it was the doing of the younger brothers[Xiahou]Zizangand[Xiahou]Zijiang.
TheChronicles of Jinstates: In the second year of theera [266], theDistrict Marquisof Gao’an, Xiahou Zuo, died. He was the grandson of[Xiahou] Dun. The succession died out. An edict stated:
”[Xiahou] Duncontributed paramount merits toWei, with them being recorded on bamboo and silk. Of old, there were no sacrifices offered to Tingjian, and there were some who lamented such.5It is all the more so then that we, having received the abdication ofWei, might forget such glorious officials. We feel obligated to select a close relation of[Xiahou] Dun,[Xiahou] Shao, to beenfeoffed.”•••
Gao Yao [皋陶], styled Tingjian [庭堅], was a minister who lived before theXia dynasty, and is considered to be legendary. He was said to be one of the greatest judicial officials in history, his fair rulings helping bring stability to the realm. The State of Liao [蓼国] was a fief granted to his descendants, but they did not offer sacrifices to him. Their destruction by the State of Chu was said to be for that reason, they did not have enough virtue.↩
***
Han Haowas fromHenei.Shi HuanofPei stateand[Han] Haowere both famous for their loyalty and bravery.[Han] Haowould attain the rank ofCentral Army Protectorwhile[Shi] Huanwould attain the rank ofArmy Leader of the Center. Both of them would lead the palace soldiers and were enfeoffed asRanged Marquises.F
TheBook of Weistates:Han Haowas styledYuansi. During the end of the Han, there were many rebellions. His county was near hills and marshes, and there were many bandits, so[Han] Haoassembled an army under him to be the protectors of the county. TheGrand Administrator,Wang Kuang, made him serve asClerk, and had him lead troops to opposeDong ZhuoatMeng ford. At the time, the maternal uncle of[Han] Hao,Du Yang, served asPrefectofHeyin, so[Dong] Zhuoseized him to summon[Han] Hao, but[Han] Haodid not follow.Yuan Shuheard about such and admired him, so he made himCavalry Commandant.Xiahou Dunheard about his reputation, invited him to a meeting, and regarded him as very extraordinary. He had him lead troops to follow on expeditions and conquests. At the time, there was a great discussion on losses and profits, and[Han] Haobelieved that the fields should be focused on. TheGrand Progenitorsaw this as good and promoted him toArmy Protector.
When theGrand Progenitorwanted to invadeLiucheng,Army LeaderShi Huanbelieved that the roads went in too deep and far, and that the plan was not comprehensive. He wanted to protest alongside[Han] Hao, but[Han] Haosaid, “As of the present, our military strength is powerful and mighty, our authority surging within theFour Seas. In fighting, we triumph, and in attacking, we capture, and there is nothing that has not gone against our will. If we do not use this time to eliminate the realm of a scourge, there will be trouble in the future. Furthermore,His Excellencyis divine in strength and whenever he acts, he has no miscalculations. We are both leaders of the Central Army and it would be inappropriate for us to halt the soldiers.” He thus followed and won inLiucheng. His rank was shifted toCentral Army Protectorand he could establish his ownChief ClerkandMajor. He followed to invadeZhang Lu, and[Zhang] Lusurrendered. Advisors reasoned the wisdom and strategies of[Han] Haowould be sufficient to stabilize the frontier, so they wanted him to have him remain to supervise the various armies in guardingHanzhong. TheGrand Progenitorsaid, “How I can be without theArmy Protector?” Thus, they returned. He was trusted so closely in such a manner. When he died, theGrand Progenitorfelt much pity. He had no sons, so his adopted son,[Han] Rong, succeeded him.
Shi Huanwas styledGongliu. When he was young, he was chivalrous and possessed a heroic spirit. When theGrand Progenitorrose up, he [Shi Huan] followed as a retainer and became actingColonel of the Central Army, following in the expeditions and conquests, and often supervising generals. He was intimate and trusted. He was transferred to becomeArmy Leader of the Center. In the fourteenth year [209], he died, and his son,[Shi] Jing, succeeded him.

Xiahou Dun [AD ?-220], styled Yuanrang, was born in Qiao county in Pei prefecture. He was a descendant of Xiahou Ying [?-172 BC]. At the age of 14 he pursued his studies under the guidance of a local teacher. There was an incident where someone had insulted his teacher, and Dun killed him. For this he was known as a righteous but violent person.
When Cao Cao first rise to power, Dun had always been under his command and followed him into battle. After Cao Cao was promoted to the title of General who Displays Firmness, he appointed Dun as his military advisor, and stationed him at Baima. Dun was later promoted to the rank ofZhechong Xiaoweiand taken up the position as the governor of Dong prefecture.
When Cao Cao attacked Tao Qian, Dun was ordered to station himself at Puyang. One of Cao Cao’s commanders, Zhang Miao, took this chance to rebel and switched to Lü Bu’s side. Zhang Miao’s troops were stationed near Juancheng, and Cao Cao’s family lived in Juancheng. Therefore Dun gathered a small army and went to the rescue. On his way there he encountered Lü Bu and his troops, and they fought. Lü Bu retreated, took Puyang and seized Dun’s supplies as a result of Dun’s absence.
Lü Bu then ordered some local commanders to Dun’s camp and staged a surrender. Dun fell for the trick and was taken as hostage. The rebelling commanders asked for a large amount for a ransom and this incident startled the rest of Dun’s troops. Han Hao, one of Dun’s commanders, ordered his troops to station themselves in front of Dun’s camp and called the other commanders for a meeting. He ordered other commanders to restrict their troops in their own camps. After the other camps came under control, Han Hao went into Dun’s camp and told the rebels loudly, “You man-eating traitors, how dare you take hostage of a general. Do you still hope to walk out of this alive? Besides, we are ordered to suppress the rebels, how can we let you off lightly just because of a general?” He then turned to Dun in teary eyes and said, “There is nothing I can do to help because the law forbids it.” After saying this he ordered his men to attack the rebels. Upon seeing this, the rebelling commanders released Dun immediately and begged for pardon. “We only wanted to ask for some cash to spend,” they said. Han Hao reprimanded them on their wrong doings and executed them after that. After Dun was rescued, Cao Cao learnt about it and praised Han Hao, “Your method can be a good example for ten thousand years.” For this, Cao Cao made a new ruling: “From now on, in situations when the enemies take hostages, kill both the enemies and the hostages. Do not take the safety of the hostages into consideration.” There were no more incidents of hostages being held from thenon.A
Pei Songzhi’s notes: According to Sun Sheng, during the time of Emperor Guangwu, 9th year of Jianwu [AD 33], robbers captured Lady Yin’s mother and younger brother. The authorities tried to make the robbers release the hostages by force, and the robbers killed the hostages. But attacking the hijackers and the hostage was an ancient ruling. Since the time of Emperor An [AD 107-125] and Emperor Shun [AD 126-144], the situation had worsened to the extent that even nobles were sometimes kidnapped, and the authorities did not follow this ruling and kidnappers escaped when they had hostages. Han Hao revived this rule so that Emperor Wei Wu [Cao Cao] praised him for this. <return>
After Cao Cao returned from Xuzhou, Dun followed him to attack Lü Bu. He was hit by an arrow and lost his left eye.BHe was later promoted to the governor of Chenliu and governor of Jiyin, and given the title ofJianwu Jiangjun, and was knighted as the duke of Gao An Xiang. During his term of office there were sometimes drought and locust attacks. Dun blockaded the river and used the fertile riverbed for agricultural purposes. He led his men to join in the labor and he carried soil on his back like the peasants. The people benefited because of his leadership, and later Dun became the governor of He’nan.
Pei Songzhi’s notes: According to the book “Wei Lue,” both Xiahou Yuan and Xiahou Dun were generals. The soldiers called Xiahou Dun “the Blind Xiahou,” and Xiahou Dun loathed this nickname. He looked at himself in the mirror and was outraged, and threw the mirror to the ground.
After Cao Cao conquered He’bei and was appointed Jiande General, he was met with resistance from the rear from the Da Jiangjun [General-in-Chief, i.e., Yuan Shao]. After the city of Ye was conquered [from Yuan Shao], Dun was promoted toFubo Jiangjunand still governed He’nan. He was given the power to impose or introduce whatever ruling he saw necessary, and was not restricted by law. In the 12th year of Jian’an [AD 207], Dun’s contributions were officially recorded, and he was given another 1800 households under his command besides the 2500 household he had already. In the 21st year of Jian’an [AD 216], after he returned with Cao Cao from war against Sun Quan, he was given the command of 26 army units and was ordered to station himself at Juchao. Cao Cao rewarded him with famous musicians and dancers, saying “Wei Jiang had united the barbaric tribes and had his legends inscribed in metal and stones, but you deserve more than that.”
In the 24th year of Jian’an [AD 219], Cao Cao’s army defeated [the army of] Liu Bei [i.e. Guan Yu] at Mo’pi. Cao Cao often had Dun ride in the same carriage with him, and Dun was allowed to visit Cao Cao in his living quarters. Cao Cao showed special intimacy, trust and high regards toward Dun, with which the rest of his generals could not compare. Dun was appointed General of the Van.CDun supervised the units returning to Shouchun. After that he was being stationed as Zhaoling. After Wei Wen Di [Cao Pi] inherited the title of Prince of Wei, Xiahou Dun was appointed as “Great General.” He passed away a few months after his new appointment. Although Xiahou Dun was a military man, he personally invited his teacher to his camp for guidance so that he can carry on his studies. He was a prudent man to himself and generous to others. He did not enjoy keeping a wealthy estate so whenever he had extra income he gave them to the masses, and whenever there was a shortage he took from the treasury. He was given the honorable title of “loyal duke” after his death.
Pei Songzhi’s notes: According to the ‘Book of Wei’, the commanders under Cao Cao all received title from Wei, except for Xiahou Dun [instead, he received his titles from the Han emperor]. Dun therefore requested for a Wei title to show his loyalty. Cao Cao said, “I heard that the best rulers learn from his officials like a student. The next best rulers become friends with his officials. Officials are men with great abilities. A small state like Wei is not worthy for a good official like you.” Dun insisted, and Cao Cao finally agreed and appointed him the position of General of the Van.
His son Xiahou Chong inherited his titles and positions. Wei Wen Di remembered Dun’s contributions and wanted to offer knighthood to his sons and grandsons. He gave Dun’s family another 1000 households, and gave all seven of Dun’s sons and two of Dun’s grandsons the title of “Marquis of the Imperial Domain.” Dun’s younger brother Xiahou Lian and Dun’s son Xiahou Mao were already dukes when he died. Initially, Cao Cao had married his daughter, Princess Qinghe, to Xiahou Mao. Mao had held the positions of palace attendant, Imperial Secretariat, and Anxi Zhendong Jiangjun. He was also given the prestigious royal mace.DAfter Xiahou Chong’s death, his son Xiahou Yi inherited Xiahou Dun’s title as the 3rd generation duke of Gao An Xiang. After Xiahou Yi’s death, his son Xiahou Shao inherited thetitle.E
Pei Songzhi’s notes: According to ‘Wei Lue’, Xiahou Mao, styled Zilin, is the middle son of Xiahou Dun. Wen Di and Mao were good friends since their younger days. After Wen Di ascended the throne as Emperor of Wei, he appointed Mao as Anxi General and gave him the royal mace, and took over Xiahou Yuan’s position as the Governor of Guanzhong. Mao was not exceptionally good in strategy and military tactics, and preferred enjoying an easy life. In the 2nd year of Taihe [AD 228], Ming Di [Cao Rui] wanted to attack Shu. Mao was appointed the position of Imperial Secretariat. When Mao was on the western front, he had kept quite a number of beautiful dancers and singers. Princess Qinghe was displeased and their relationship turned sour because of this. Later on Xiahou Mao’s brothers had behaved impolitely, and Mao reprimanded them harshly. Fearing that Mao would punish them they conspired with the princess and accused Mao of slandering the court and the Emperor. The princess sent this accusation to the Emperor and a imperial edict was issued to arrest Mao. Ming Di wanted to kill Mao and asked court official Duan Mo for his opinions. Duan Mo said, “It is obvious that this is a false accusation because the princess is not in good terms with Mao. Besides, Fubo Jiangjun [Xiahou Dun] and the late Emperor had jointly laid the foundations of this empire, please think thrice should your highness decide to convict Mao.” Ming Di calmed down and said, “This is what I had in mind, too.” He issued an imperial edict to find out who wrote the complaint for the princess, and it turned out to be a false accusation by Xiahou Mao’s nephews, Xiahou Zang and Xiahou Jiang.
Pei Songzhi’s notes: According to ‘Jin Yang Qiu,’ in the 2nd year of Taishi [AD 266] the duke of Gao An Xiang, Xiahou Zuo, the grandson of Xiahou Dun, passed away. The family line ended here and the title was taken back by the court. Sima Yan’s edict said, ‘Xiahou Dun made great contributions to the Wei empire, and will be remembered in historical records. It’s a pity that his family line ended here. Besides, I inherited the throne from the Wei empire, how can I forget these great pioneers of Wei? It is necessary to find Xiahou Dun’s close relatives and present them with honorable titles.’
